perturbation.original_text: 1. "CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION" as used in this Agreement means all technical or business information disclosed by one of the Parties to another pursuant to the JRA that is identified at the time of disclosure or within thirty (30) days thereafter as being confidential and proprietary.
perturbation.changed_text: 1. "CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION" as used in this Agreement means any information disclosed by one of the Parties to another pursuant to the JRA, irrespective of whether it is identified as confidential or proprietary at the time of disclosure or within thirty (30) days thereafter.
perturbation.explanation: This removes the requirement for the information to be identified as confidential at the time of disclosure or within 30 days, potentially conflicting with trade secret law which often requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text states that all information is considered confidential, even if not explicitly identified as such, potentially contradicting trade secret law that requires active measures to maintain secrecy. New York law requires efforts to maintain secrecy to qualify for trade secret protection.

Privileged communications; electronic communication thereof. No communication privileged under this article shall lose its privileged character for the sole reason that it is communicated by electronic means or because persons necessary for the delivery or facilitation of such electronic communication may have access to the content of the communication.
perturbation.justification: The changed text eliminates the requirement to identify information as confidential, which contradicts the principle that trade secrets require active measures to maintain secrecy. The Law Explanation specifies that New York law necessitates efforts to maintain secrecy for trade secret protection. While the provided scraped snippets don't directly address trade secret law, the Law Explanation, coupled with the Perturbation Explanation, clearly establishes the contradiction. The original text provided a clear definition of "Confidential Information", while the changed text undermines the ability to legally protect trade secrets by removing the need to identify them as such.

perturbation.original_text: No information will be regarded as CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION if the Party to which it is disclosed can show by competent proof that such information (a) was at the time of disclosure, or subsequently became, through no fault of the receiving Party, known to the general public through publication or otherwise; or (b) was, subsequent to disclosure to a Party, lawfully and independently received by that Party from a third party who had the right to disclose it without restriction.
perturbation.changed_text: No information will be regarded as CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION if the Party to which it is disclosed asserts that such information (a) was at the time of disclosure, or subsequently became, through no fault of the receiving Party, known to the general public through publication or otherwise; or (b) was, subsequent to disclosure to a Party, independently received by that Party from a third party.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the words 'lawfully' and 'who had the right to disclose it without restriction' and changing 'can show by competent proof' to 'asserts that', this modification makes it far easier for a receiving party to claim information is not confidential, even if the third party was not authorized to disclose it, weakening the protection of the confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text allows a party to merely 'assert' that information was independently received, without providing proof that the third party had the right to disclose it. This undermines the legal standard for establishing that information is not a trade secret, which typically requires demonstrating lawful acquisition.

Privileged communications; electronic communication thereof. No communication privileged under this article shall lose its privileged character for the sole reason that it is communicated by electronic means or because persons necessary for the delivery or facilitation of such electronic communication may have access to the content of the communication.
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts legal principles related to the protection of confidential information and trade secrets. The original text required 'competent proof' that information was publicly known or lawfully received from a third party with the right to disclose it. The modified text weakens this protection by only requiring the receiving party to 'assert' that the information was independently received, removing the 'lawfully' requirement and the need for proof. The NYSenate.gov snippet does not pertain directly to trade secrets, but removing the need for 'competent proof' lowers the bar for claiming information is not confidential and undermines the protection normally afforded to trade secrets or confidential information, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Specific aspects or details of CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION shall not be deemed to be within the public domain or in the possession of a Party merely because the CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION is embraced by general disclosures in the public domain or in the possession of a Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Specific aspects or details of CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION shall be deemed to be within the public domain or in the possession of a Party if the CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION is embraced by general disclosures in the public domain or in the possession of a Party.
perturbation.explanation: This change directly contradicts the original statement, weakening the protection of specific details within the Confidential Information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text says that details of confidential information are considered public domain if the general idea is public. This weakens the trade secret protection afforded to the specific details and implementation of the secret, contrary to the intent of protecting specific aspects of the confidential information.

Privileged communications; electronic communication thereof. No communication privileged under this article shall lose its privileged character for the sole reason that it is communicated by electronic means or because persons necessary for the delivery or facilitation of such electronic communication may have access to the content of the communication.
perturbation.justification: The change in text directly contradicts the original intent. The original text explicitly states that specific aspects of confidential information are NOT in the public domain merely because the general information is. The changed text reverses this, stating specific aspects ARE in the public domain if the general idea is. This weakens the protection of confidential information, undermining the original contract's intent to protect specific details. The LLM Law Explanation supports this, stating it weakens trade secret protection afforded to specific details.

perturbation.original_text: 3. In consideration of each and every disclosure of CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION, the Parties agree to: (a) treat as confidential and to preserve the confidentiality of all CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION; (b) use any and all CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION solely in connection with the performance of the IRA and for no other purpose; (c) make no disclosures of any CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION to any party other than officers and employees of a Party to this IRA; (d) limit access to CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION to those officers and employees having a reasonable need for such INFORMATION and being boUnd by a written obligation to maintain the confidentiality of such INFORMATION; and (e) maintain in confidence any information regarding the nature or scope of any transaction between the Parties, except to the extent such information must be disclosed pursuant to law, and then only after notifying the other Party of such requirement.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. In consideration of each and every disclosure of CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION, the Parties agree to: (a) treat as confidential and to endeavor to preserve the confidentiality of all CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION; (b) use any and all CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION solely in connection with the performance of the IRA and for no other purpose, except for internal research purposes; (c) make no disclosures of any CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION to any party other than officers and employees of a Party to this IRA and their consultants; (d) limit access to CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION to those officers and employees having a need for such INFORMATION; and (e) maintain in confidence any information regarding the nature or scope of any transaction between the Parties.
perturbation.explanation: This change weakens the obligation to preserve confidentiality, expands the allowed uses of confidential information, and broadens the scope of permissible disclosures, undermining the agreement's confidentiality protections.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text allows for 'endeavoring' to preserve confidentiality instead of a strict obligation, adds an exception for 'internal research purposes', and allows disclosure to 'consultants' without specifying a written obligation. These weaken the legal protections afforded to trade secrets under NY law, making enforcement more difficult.

Privileged communications; electronic communication thereof. No communication privileged under this article shall lose its privileged character for the sole reason that it is communicated by electronic means or because persons necessary for the delivery or facilitation of such electronic communication may have access to the content of the communication.
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens confidentiality obligations by using 'endeavoring' instead of 'preserve the confidentiality'. It broadens permissible uses of confidential information by adding 'except for internal research purposes,' and it expands disclosures to 'consultants' without requiring a written confidentiality obligation. This directly contradicts the initial agreement which aimed to protect confidential information stringently, potentially undermining its enforceability.

perturbation.original_text: 5. The obligations of each and every Party, and each employee and officer of each Party Under this Agreement will expire five (5) years from the termination of the JRA.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. The obligations of each and every Party, and each employee and officer of each Party Under this Agreement will expire one (1) year from the termination of the JRA.
perturbation.explanation: Reduces the period of confidentiality to one year, which could be insufficient to protect trade secrets, especially in industries with long product lifecycles.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not directly violating the statute, shortening the confidentiality period makes it harder to argue that reasonable efforts were made to protect the trade secret for its useful life. NY law requires that the trade secret holder take active and ongoing measures to protect the information.

No communication privileged under this article shall lose its privileged character for the sole reason that it is communicated by electronic means or because persons necessary for the delivery or facilitation of such electronic communication may have access to the content of the communication.
perturbation.justification: The original contract sets a five-year confidentiality period, while the changed version reduces it to one year. The Law Explanation states that a shorter confidentiality period makes it harder to prove reasonable efforts to protect a trade secret, potentially conflicting with NY law requiring active and ongoing protection. Although the scraped snippets do not directly address confidentiality periods, the Law Explanation suggests this change could weaken trade secret protection, which the Misaligned Terminology definition recognizes as creating a legal contradiction.
